title: Cancer saps strength from immune cells
html_body:
What matters in cancer research |
View this email in your browser Thursday 9 April 2026

Hello Nature readers,
This week, we discover why immune cells stop functioning inside tumours, hear that pig semen is being used to treat eye cancer and learn that autoimmune disease could be caused by ‘the cancer you never knew you had’.
The loss of healthy mitochondria from dendritic cells could be caused by theft or nutrient depletion by cancer cells. (Artur Plawgo/Science Photo Library)

Cancer saps strength from immune cells

As tumours grow, immune cells called dendritic cells are weakened by a loss of healthy mitochondria. Without these energy-producing organelles, dendritic cells don’t perform their crucial role of displaying fragments of cancer cell proteins, and the T cells are less likely to attack the cancer. Mice with skin cancer mounted a powerful anti-tumour immune response when given dendritic cells with lots of healthy mitochondria. The mice suppressed tumour growth completely when this treatment was combined with a type of immunotherapy called a checkpoint inhibitor that makes cancer cells more vulnerable to attack by T cells.

Nature | 4 min read
 Reference: Science paper (2 April)

Drops made from pig semen treat eye cancer

Eye drops that contain drug-carrying molecules derived from pig semen can stop the growth of a retinal cancer in mice without affecting the animals’ vision. The drops contain tiny particles called exosomes that can penetrate the barrier around the retina so drugs can kill tumour cells without causing damage to the surrounding tissue. Researchers hope that the drops could be developed into a kinder treatment for children with retinoblastoma, which is currently treated with injections into the eye, chemotherapy or laser therapy.

Nature | 4 min read
 Reference: Science Advances paper (27 March)

Persistent viral load drives blood cancer

People who carry Epstein-Barr virus but don’t have symptoms of an infection are more likely to develop a type of blood cancer known as Hodgkin’s lymphoma. Researchers spotted the association while looking for 31 common viruses inside blood or saliva samples from more than 900,000 healthy participants using whole-genome sequencing. “Antivirals … may be worth evaluation for potential prophylactic benefit against Hodgkin’s lymphoma,” write the study’s authors.

ScienceAlert | 4 min read
 Reference: Nature paper (25 March)

ADVERTISEMENT FEATURE
St. Jude investigator Mark Hatley, who encountered the unsolved case of DICER1 predisposition early in his career, pictured with graduate student Randy Larsen, who solved the cold case decades later. (St. Jude Children’s Research Hospital)

How the DICER1 gene casts a NET for cancer

St. Jude researchers have cracked a longstanding mystery around why children with mutations in the DICER1 gene are prone to a rare soft tissue cancer called rhabdomyosarcoma. The tumour cells aren’t the real offenders at all — the problem starts in the surrounding tissue. When DICER1 is faulty in the body’s normal cells, it disrupts the local environment and draws in overactive immune cells called neutrophils. In preclinical models, these immune cells responded by releasing fibrous webs made out of their own DNA called neutrophil extracellular traps (NETs). These NETs work to entrap pathogens, but the self-destructing neutrophils can also release enzymes that help tumours to grow. When researchers used an already approved drug to prevent neutrophils from rupturing, the tumour growth associated with DICER1 mutations slowed down, pointing to a potential prevention strategy.

Reference: Developmental Cell paper

Must-read papers

Why ependymoma hits boys harder

Sex hormones drive a type of childhood brain cancer called posterior fossa type A (PFA) ependymoma — leading to more cases and poorer outcomes in boys than girls. In mice, higher levels of sex hormones called androgens, which drive the development of male characteristics, promoted the growth of PFA ependymoma. Blocking these hormones diminished the proliferation of the tumour. “Anti-androgen therapies represent a potential clinical avenue to target this currently untreatable childhood cancer,” write the authors.

Reference: Nature paper (25 March)
Go deeper with this analysis by neuroscientist Najla Kfoury-Beaumont (Nature News & Views | 8 min read)
Brain cells called neuroepithelial cells differentiate more slowly in boys than girls, meaning that they retain the flexible characteristics of stem cells for longer and are more likely to become cancerous. (Nature News & Views | 8 min read)

2.5 million

The number of people invited to undergo a low-dose CT scan as part of the UK’s national lung cancer screening program between 2019 and 2025. Of those who received a scan, 1.4% were found to have lung cancer, many of which were caught at an early stage. People were invited to participate if they were aged 55-74 and had ever smoked or were at higher risk of developing lung cancer. (Nature Research Highlight | 1 min read)
Reference: Nature Medicine paper (23 March)

Features & opinion

‘The cancer you never knew you had’

Some autoimmune diseases could be a side effect of the immune system’s fight against cancer. Researchers found that some people can slow the growth of NMDA receptor-positive tumours by producing antibodies, but that these same antibodies can also trigger autoimmune brain disease. “Our findings raise the possibility that a considerable proportion of autoimmune disease might arise in individuals whose tumours have already been eliminated by the immune system — effectively, ‘the cancer you never knew you had’,” write study co-authors cancer researcher Sam Kleeman and biochemist-oncologist Tobias Janowitz.

Nature Research Briefing | 7 min read
 Reference: Nature paper (25 March)

How the body overrules ‘selfish’ genes

Genes only exist today because they were good at replicating themselves in the past. But dial up this tendency, and ‘selfish’ genes can harm the organism as a whole. The genome balances the survival drive of individual genes with the needs of the organism by operating like a ‘parliament’ where majority rules, write evolutionary theorists Thomas Scott and Stuart West in The Paradox of the Organism, a collection of essays that explore such conflicts. Cancers are evolutionary ‘cheaters’ that escape this regulatory control, they write. “I came away feeling a pleasant discomfort at how complicated the topic is, but enlightened all the same”, writes computational biologist C. Brandon Ogbunugafor in his review of the book.

Nature | 6 min read

Infographic of the week
The intestines of mice (shown above) retain an epigenetic memory for more than 100 days after repeated cycles of inflammation and healing, and are more prone to developing tumours during this time. Researchers used a chemical called low-dose dextran sodium sulfate to injure the intestines and create three states of disease progression: acute injury, chronic injury and recovery.
Reference: Nature paper (25 March) (Nagaraja, S., Ojeda-Miron, L., Zhang, R. et al./Nature)

Term of the week

Commercial determinants of health

The world’s population has been increasingly exposed to cancer risk factors like tobacco, air pollution, alcohol and processed foods over the past 30 years, largely as a result of increasing commercial activity. “Transnational corporations that manufacture and market health-harming products are a primary vector for the global increase in mortality,” writes the University of California, San Francisco’s Consortium of the Center to End Corporate Harm. (The New England Journal of Medicine | 14 min read)

Earlier this week, four astronauts went to the far side of the moon, losing contact with Earth for about 40 minutes. Then the crew witnessed an eclipse from space. At times, they were lost for words. “There’s no adjectives,” said commander Reid Wiseman. “I’m going to need to invent some new ones to describe what we are looking at out this window.” So, naturally now I’m wondering if NASA would consider taking a journalist into space…

Radio tips and feedback to cancerbriefing@nature.com.

Thanks for reading,
Felicity Nelson, editor, Nature Briefing: Cancer
With contributions by Jacob Smith
